slides.title: IETF correspondence must allow derivative works

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: gendispatch @IETF 126

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: What’s the draft about Pete?

chat.author: Jon Peterson
chat.text: i am also surprised we need to be so strict - if you don't care leave at AOB time?

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Yeah, I don't mind someone advertising something that just dropped, but discussion with an expectation that the discussion would be substantive is not on.

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: We got a lot of requests to "make a presentation" at DISPATCH. I'm a little concerned about opening the door to doing that as opposed to dispatching things.

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: I didn’t even know there is a rule that you can dispatch only once. Would „please do more work and come back“ also be a dispatch outcome?

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: As Dispatch chair, I wasn't aware of that rule either :)

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: Also "dispatch to mailing list to establish interest" should leave option to come back later to dispatch to BoF etc?

chat.author: Robert Wilton
chat.text: @Mirja, why not, but I think that it should be at the chairs discretion.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Here is what the dispatch charter says: "Precedence to present will be given to proposals which have evidence of interest in the
form of active Internet-Drafts and list discussion. An Internet-Draft shall not be
dispatched multiple times unless a substantially revised proposal is put
forth, including compelling new reasons for accepting the work." It appears gendispatch does not contain this text

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: @Andrew I would hope that discussion on the mailing list would prompt AD to support a BoF request without needing to be re-dispatched.

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: @Jim Fair

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: I didn't realize that didn't get copied over to the gendispatch charter.

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: @Eric Thanks -- guess I need to reread the charter

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: But of course that was the GEN AD, so I guess he can invent new rules

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text:
1: Yes. It's stupid, but yes.
2: No
3: Sure.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Isn't a better framing that "Participants are required to license their correspondance to permit the creation of derivative works." ?

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: Is "get it on PROCON's charter once they're done with 2026bis and 2418bis" an acceptable outcome?

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Mark Nottingham don't you have to make a decision about whether to involve counsel?

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text: If Roman says counsel will be involved, counsel will be involved.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
Martin Thomson said:

Isn't a better framing that "Participants are required to license their correspondance to permit the creation of derivative works." ?

...with a possible addendum "...for the use in further correspondance in the IETF process."

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: To be clear - we are not competent to state the problem OR the fix.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: It seems to me that perhaps we should just eliminate the explicit ability to exclude your own contributions

chat.author: Robert Wilton
chat.text: AD Sponsor - do legal counsel at the same time.

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: @EKR: Can I exclude other peoples contributions?!!! :-P

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: Indeed, AD sponsorship requires an IETF Last-Call for 4 weeks.

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: That is the IETF process

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
Eric Rescorla said:

It seems to me that perhaps we should just eliminate the explicit ability to exclude your own contributions

Yes, that's what I suggested above.

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: And for a BCP IETF consensus has always been required

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: I think that this feels more like an errata than a whole new topic. No, I don't suggest using the Errata process, but it's more of a big-fix -bis than a "here is my new theoary..."

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: +1 ekr

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Martin Thomson said:

Eric Rescorla said:

It seems to me that perhaps we should just eliminate the explicit ability to exclude your own contributions

Yes, that's what I suggested above.

And  then yeah you could put whatever stuff you want in your footer, but it has no explicit relevance in our process other than usual copyright principles.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Precisely.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: There is an actual problem hiding here, which is whether we want people to be able to do that.

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: Initially I was against it, but perhaps revisiting the no-derivitives clause does mae sense....

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: @Mark Nottingham I think what Jay just said was that the lawyers believe there is no problem here, that the current 5378 text is clear.

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: perhaps no-derivatives on some streams but not others?

chat.author: John Klensin
chat.text: I agree about what  AD sponsorship requires while also agreeing with Rich about level of attention and optics.  If we need anything other than "send to the lawyers", it should get wider and more obvious consideration than many AD sponsored drafts get in practice.  And, if the current rules are clear, maybe we need an RFC with the lawyers as authors.

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: Perhaps. And I used to strongly argue for keeping it, but if we lose  one of two "We made a protocol, this is what it looks like, no you cant change it" documents, meh...'

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: I think that some of this is that there is currently a bit of a dumpster fire around this particilar topic...

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: Terms, conditions AND our licenses are not magic spells -- they have to be interpreted in the context of the law (which includes concepts like fair use, etc.)

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: "By subscribing to this mailing list, you agree that the content of any email footer that you send will be ignored."?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: It might be useful in Lars's version of this to have the IETF say that their policy is that those corporate footers are non-actionable, and so the IETF will feel free to ingest messages regardless of those footers

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: Then you get into matters of contract formation, etc. Fun times.

chat.author: Jay Daley
chat.text: 5378 already says that

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
I think that what we want is a condition of participation that you license correspondance to permit the creation of derivative works (...optionally...: for correspondance).
Then we ask counsel for how to create the language that effects that outcome.

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: @MT don't use the words "derivative works" -- they have specific meaning

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: @Andrew Campling Footer, or header, or in the body of the text, or any statement outside of the email itself. The key is if you participate, anyone can make use of what you email in an IETF context.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Mark Nottingham fine.  I was using it in the colloquial sense, see note about asking an actual lawyer to write the concrete text.

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: "copy" is safe :)

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: "copy or excerpt"?

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: There are subtleties there but sure

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: What about the purpose limitation?  Do we need to limit the license to use in discussions here, or should it be a broader license?  I suspect that "broader" is necessary.

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: Nottnick doesn’t your draft anyway belong in procon?

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: That's likely unnecessary and might lead to unintended consequences.

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: procon has a very tight charter

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
Mark Nottingham said:

That's likely unnecessary and might lead to unintended consequences.

Not sure about the "that" here.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: You can't keep the genders private completely consistent with open selection

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: For obvious reasons

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: That = broad

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I think you might be able to have commitments to gender so it just discloses the nomcom member's genders

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
Mark Nottingham said:

That = broad

I meant, in which direction are you talking about: should we make it narrower or broader?

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: The thing that caught my eye in this draft is that we go to absurd lengths to prove the integrity of the noncom selection process, but this proposes withholding one critical factor.

chat.author: Robert Wilton
chat.text: I think that this is trying to fix gender makeup in the wrong place.  I.e., we need to get a better gender distribution into the IETF and then naturally we would end up with a better makeup of Nomcom and hopefully also in the leadership.

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: Making decisions might actually fit into „guidelines for WG adoption“ in the procon charter

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: @MT would need to dig into details about the scope of the license -- eg who it assigns to, what rights, etc. I am not paid to be a copyright lawyer (unfortunately)

chat.author: John Klensin
chat.text: What always worries me about things like this is that it launches us down a slippery slope.  If gender, why not require age and/or geographic distribution?  The company restriction seems rather different from any of those three.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: It's a bit hard to have a gendispatch discussion without getting into the merits

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: we are discussing dispatch

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: not the content

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: rigth?

chat.author: John Levine
chat.text: Assuming this is supposed to improve the gender balance of nomcom selectees, how could you tell if it worked?

chat.author: Mallory Knodel
chat.text: I'm happy for feedback on content

chat.author: Martin Thomson
Grace Rachmany
chat.text: For me there's a question about what we are representing. Should it be representation that reflects IETF participants? Should it be representation of the geographic makeup of the technology vendors? Should it be representation of the Internet users? I am not sure how to think of this.

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: I suspect I'm dumb, but I don't really undestand how this would run in process. We pick N people randomly, and then ask gender, and if all are X, then we throw out the last person, rerun and ask the next selected until we get someone !X?

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: The process for including someone of the non-dominant gender needs to be thought through. It's an inclusion, not an exclusion like the limit on nomcom members from the same employer so it needs to work differently and be described precisely.

chat.author: John Klensin
chat.text: I'd much rather see more efforts to encourage multi-gender participation in the IETF and reducing barriers for volunteering for the Nomcom, then letting the randomization process work.

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: Agree with Martin: I thought the original was a simple solution to an ongoing problem. The comportment part makes me uncomfortable.

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: the NomCom selects among the pool of volunteers randomly -- I don't see how this changes pose a risk in giving you a less capable Nomcom

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: *these

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: And haven't we then exposed the gender of everyone who is next in the list who is selected but not appointed?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I think technically you could build something that just discloses the genders of the people being selected or skipped.

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: @EKR: Ok, so I guess I did understand. That might be fine...

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Mallory, can you please try to address the question more directly?

chat.author: John Levine
chat.text: Amazingly, I agree with JCK. We need to increase the volunteer pool.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Basically if everyone had to disclose their gender to the nomcom chair. That chair published a commitment for everyone, and then each person examined had a revealed commitment

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: We could use DAP!

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: I wonder how many people that sweeps up....

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Warren Kumari said:

I wonder how many people that sweeps up....

Typically about 15

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: So, everyone selected on the nomcom, and then number of additional people until you hit someone who is not X = ~15

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Yes, and note that this would increase the number

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: That's certainly one way. The draft doesn't define the mechanism, and as long as it is repeatable by outsiders there are several options.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Rich Salz said:

That's certainly one way. The draft doesn't define the mechanism, and as long as it is repeatable by outsiders there are several options.

Yes, I'm just designing on the fly

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Maybe we could run an iterative version of the socialist millionaire's protocol?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: The very minimal thing is a verifiable proof that the conditions are met

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: There's an ietf-nomcom list "for revisions to the nomcom process". That seems like the right place to discuss this further.

chat.author: Mallory Knodel
chat.text: Good idea Jim

chat.author: Grace Rachmany
chat.text: It might be smart to do some research to find out why certain people don't run for leadership positions. That way we could really understand where the problem starts. If we are forcing people to step in when they don't want to, or if they needed more funding to step into such positions, or .... etc. Without knowing why we don't have as many nominees of certain genders, I think we are addressing a symptom.

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: +! dispatch to nomcom mailing list

chat.author: Roman Danyliw
chat.text: The standing list we have to discuss NomCom eligibility is eligibility-discuss@ietf.org

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I think it needs chairing

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: another example is perhaps going by pronouns isn't BEST practice, but maybe it's GOOD ENOUGH to meet the goal.

chat.author: Mallory Knodel
chat.text: if something better, let's do that then

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: I'm not actually proposing this here, but I've long felt that it would be good for the list of people who have been nominated be kept quiet for a few weeks -- I have often not nominated soneone because I see that they are already nominated... and I think that nomination is a strong signal. Do others think this is worth my writing up?

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: Grace, there is a paper by Merike https://www.ietf.org/blog/report-experience-of-women-participating-in-the-ietf/

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: I am not sure that NomCOM list is useful, having this discussion run in parallel with the work of the nomcom 2026 just does not seem reasonable

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: I like the idea of this being AD sponsored

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: eligibility-discuss would be a fine place too. I was hoping we didn't need to create yet another mailing list.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Carolina Caeiro said:

I am not sure that NomCOM list is useful, having this discussion run in parallel with the work of the nomcom 2026 just does not seem reasonable

I don't see how the work cannot be run in parallel with a nomcom, given the length of the process and that about 1/2 the year is the nomcom process

chat.author: Lars Eggert
chat.text: Since the mic line is closed: We had good experience with short-lived WGs to update NomCom-related processes. We should do that here; even for the "simple" change of doing a gender bar.

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: It also removes the "I won't run because $incumbant has already been nominated" issue...

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: Pivoting the NOMCOM to something that is conceptualised as a hiring committee would require a WG

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: We seem to be spending a lot of time discussing content, not so much on dispatch....

chat.author: John Klensin
chat.text: @Jim   +1.

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: @Carolina ietf-nomcom isn't actually used by nomcom. Archive says it has been idle since 2024.

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text:
Verifying that the requirement is satisified is a matter of 2 people selected agreeing to publish a statement that their gender is different.
It's the implicit reveal that the 5 skipped persons had declared the same gender as the first 9 selectees that rankles.

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: @Andrew: +1

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: ... because it's a different work mode / body of practice, even if the functions are similar.

chat.author: Grace Rachmany
chat.text: Thanks, Carolina, the research for me does point out that there is probably more low-hanging fruit than changes to Noncom, which is optimistic-- there are easier ways to address this.

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: @Harald: Yes ,that was what I was meaning (earlier)

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text: The "use structured interview questions" definitely belongs in Nomcom operating procedures, not in the BCP, in my mind.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Harald Alvestrand said:

Verifying that the requirement is satisified is a matter of 2 people selected agreeing to publish a statement that their gender is different.
It's the implicit reveal that the 5 skipped persons had declared the same gender as the first 9 selectees that rankles.

And note that all people would be required to provide their gender to the nomcom chair or whatever.

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: That's a good point Lars, a short lived WG, AD sponsored

chat.author: Lars Eggert
chat.text: Dispatch to new WG, work items at least the gender bar, with the option to recharter for the other things in the I-D if the WG (and IETF) have consensus for that

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: I don't understand "a short lived WG, AD sponsored"... ?

chat.author: Lars Eggert
chat.text: @Carolina when I say "WG" I explicitly mean "not AD-sponsored" though

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: Those feel directly in conflit.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: As a general matter, I don't think that ADs should sponsor drafts outside of their area.

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text: ADs sponsor WGs, don't they? I think that was a tongue-slipl.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: My experience with NomCom is that those comportment items are generally followed by choice (of the chairs and the group as a whole). That makes a statute like this less necessary.  On the other hand, a requirement that governs what happens in confidence within a NomCom could be counterproductive.

chat.author: John Levine
chat.text: @Martin Thomson agreed. When I was the ISOC process observer, I saw the nomcom try hard to do a good job.

chat.author: Benson Muite
chat.text: There appears to be other interest in NomCom changes https://richsalz.github.io/nomcom-changes/draft-rsalz-gih-nomcom-2years.html so a WG would be helpful.

chat.author: John Klensin
chat.text: @Lars, we don't need to work it out today, but I'd be very hesitant to see a WG narrowly focused on this one issue with "recharter later".   That is exactly how we get ourselves into slipperly scope problems.

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text: https://github.com/ietf-wg-elegy

chat.author: Tara Tarakiyee
chat.text: @mallory: Its hard to find a reference specific to hiring committees but there's related research on corporate boards: https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=1858347

chat.author: Mallory Knodel
chat.text: ty tara

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text: Resurrecting WGs is allowed.

chat.author: Roman Danyliw
chat.text: The closed WG ELEGY =  https://datatracker.ietf.org/wg/elegy/about/.  Please use this existing mailing = https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/browse/eligibility-discuss/

chat.author: Rich Salz
chat.text: Re https://richsalz.github.io/nomcom-changes/draft-rsalz-gih-nomcom-2years.html that was pretty definitively "no". But if someone else wants to champion it, take the text :)

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I mean obviously, we all got nerdsniped into building a system that tried to be verifiable but still produce this result :)

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: Given random selection, it's not possible that someone from the list is unacceptable in any category.

chat.author: Wendy Seltzer
chat.text: Affirmative action is not "some people are more equal than others."

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Pete Resnick said:

Given random selection, it's not possible that someone from the list is unacceptable in any category.

Say again?

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text: I don't want to dive into the question of whether "prefer not to say" is a different gender from other genders, but I'm sure someone will bring it up.

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: @Chair we have dispatch outcome, useful to move on before we time out?

chat.author: Robert Wilton
chat.text: If there are not many nomcom volunteers of a particular gender, and we require 1 or 2 to be selected, then they are much more likely to be selected, which may make them less likely to volunteer for Nomcom in the first place.   Is it possible that this might then create a different problem with volunteers?

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text:
Eric Rescorla said:

Pete Resnick said:

Given random selection, it's not possible that someone from the list is unacceptable in any category.

Say again?

If they are on the list, they are deemed acceptable in that they've met the qualifications. If we choose a person from that list on the basis of gender, that can't possible mean that they are "unacceptable along other bases" as John seemed to indicate.

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: Another slipppery slope dimension: inclusion of non-native English speakers?

chat.author: Tom Newton
chat.text: Well, that neatly solved the problem of not presenting other things :joy:

chat.author: John Klensin
chat.text: @Jim.  A far better criterion to look at than the examples I used.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Pete Resnick said:

Eric Rescorla said:

Pete Resnick said:

Given random selection, it's not possible that someone from the list is unacceptable in any category.

Say again?

If they are on the list, they are deemed acceptable in that they've met the qualifications. If we choose a person from that list on the basis of gender, that can't possible mean that they are "unacceptable along other bases" as John seemed to indicate.

Ah, yes, I agree with that, as long as we restrict "acceptable" to local judgements rather than those of the group collectively (e.g., the 2-per company rule)

chat.author: Robert Wilton
chat.text: Yes to "continute to start"

chat.author: Grace Rachmany
chat.text: It would be super fun to write the draft in a lore-like format.
